preamble: The voiced palatal plosive /ɟ/ can undergo a significant phonetic transformation, weakening to become the close front rounded vowel /y/. This shift represents a substantial degree of lenition, where a stop consonant vocalizes into a vowel, often involving intermediate stages of frication and approximation.
phoneticEffects: Lenition, vocalization, palatalization (inherent in the source consonant)

examples.note: 'I looked after'. This demonstrates the weakening of /ɟ/ to /y/.
'this side'. Part of a phrase 'this side that side', showing /ɟ/ to /y/.
'that side'. Part of a phrase 'this side that side', showing /ɟ/ to /y/.
'what'. This example, while starting with /d/, is cited in the context of palatal weakening to /y/, suggesting an intermediate palatalized /dʲ/ or /ɟ/ stage.

sources: Khan, G. (2016). The Neo-Aramaic Dialect of The Assyrian Christians of Urmi I: Phonology and Morphology. Brill.
